approaches.name: DFS with Cycle Detection
approaches.description: Explicit cycle detection using DFS with parent tracking

approaches.name: Mathematical Property
approaches.description: Leverage graph theory: connected + (n-1 edges) = tree

keyInsights.description: Connected graph with exactly n-1 edges cannot have cycles by mathematical impossibility
keyInsights.ahaeMoment: Realizing that mathematical properties can eliminate algorithmic complexity entirely
keyInsights.practicalValue: Transforms O(V+E) cycle detection into simple edge count check

keyInsights.description: Checking edges.length !== n-1 eliminates most invalid cases instantly
keyInsights.mathematicalBasis: Tree theorem: connected graph with n nodes has exactly n-1 edges
keyInsights.practicalValue: Early termination saves computation on invalid inputs

keyInsights.description: Must track parent to avoid false cycle detection when naturally revisiting nodes
keyInsights.commonPitfall: Without parent tracking, immediate false positive cycle detection

keyInsights.description: Show both algorithmic competence and mathematical insight
keyInsights.interviewValue: Demonstrates comprehensive understanding and mathematical maturity
keyInsights.practicalTip: Start with mathematical approach, then show algorithmic understanding

mistakesMade.lesson: Be careful with assignment operators in conditionals

mistakesMade.description: Used one parent variable for entire DFS instead of tracking per-node
mistakesMade.lesson: Each node needs its own parent context in DFS

mistakesMade.description: Initially thought any revisited node means cycle
mistakesMade.lesson: In undirected graphs, revisiting parent is normal
mistakesMade.howFixed: Drew small graph examples and traced DFS manually

mistakesMade.description: Implemented complex cycle detection before considering mathematical properties
mistakesMade.lesson: Always check if mathematical properties can simplify the problem
mistakesMade.howFixed: Discovered n-1 edges theorem eliminates need for explicit cycle detection

improvements: More confident with graph theory theorems

coreInsight: Sometimes the most elegant solution comes from leveraging mathematical properties rather than implementing complex algorithms. Connected + exactly n-1 edges = guaranteed tree.
constraintPattern: Graph constraints often have mathematical shortcuts that eliminate algorithmic complexity
nextSteps: Apply similar mathematical thinking to other graph problems
interviewTips: Always check if mathematical properties can simplify the problem
Show multiple approaches to demonstrate comprehensive understanding
Explain the mathematical reasoning behind edge count requirements
Handle edge cases (n=0, n=1) early and explicitly
Start with the elegant mathematical solution, then show algorithmic depth
Draw small examples to verify your logic during the interview
Mention the fundamental tree theorem: connected + (n-1 edges) = tree
Demonstrate both theoretical knowledge and practical coding skills

learnings: Graph theory theorems as practical tools

testingStrategy.insight: Test cases helped catch both implementation bugs and edge case oversights
